news_summary: Innovative advancements are accelerating quantum computing development, while efforts are underway to better connect impactful Global South solutions in health and energy to the communities that need them most.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées innovantes accélèrent le développement de l'informatique quantique, tandis que des efforts sont déployés pour mieux mettre en relation les solutions porteuses issues des pays du Sud dans les domaines de la santé et de l'énergie avec les communautés qui en ont le plus besoin.
news_summary_es: Los avances innovadores están acelerando el desarrollo de la computación cuántica, al tiempo que se están llevando a cabo iniciativas para conectar mejor las soluciones del Sur Global —de gran repercusión en los ámbitos de la salud y la energía— con las comunidades que más las necesitan.

articles.title: This simple twist could bring quantum computers closer to reality
articles.summary: Researchers found that twisting layered sheets of hexagonal boron nitride can dramatically change the light produced by quantum emitters embedded within the material. The technique offers an unexpected new level of control over components that could power future quantum computers, communications systems, and sensors.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Technology Sydney have demonstrated a new way to control tiny sources of quantum light by twisting atomically thin layers of hexagonal boron nitride.

The advance provides scientists with a new method for tuning quantum emitters, which are microscopic light sources that could play an important role in future technologies such as quantum computing, secure communications, and ultra-sensitive sensors.

Lead author Dr. Angus Gale said the work offers researchers a valuable new tool for making these quantum systems more practical.

"You can measure these quantum emitters and see that they exist, but it's hard to make them work in practice. This gives us a lever to get closer to that -- a step towards the realization of quantum technologies," said Dr. Gale.

Twisting Layers Changes Quantum Light

During the experiments, Gale and his team found that twisting the material could significantly alter both the color and wavelength of the light emitted by the quantum emitters. The magnitude of the change was especially noteworthy.

Most studies create a device at a specific twist angle and leave it unchanged. In contrast, the researchers were able to repeatedly lift, rotate, and restack the material, allowing them to continuously modify its properties.

"We're leveraging the fact that this material, hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), is layered. We can pick it up, stack it, twist it, and use that twist to modify the emitters. You can't really do that with traditional materials like diamond or silicon carbide."

"The benefit is that we used this twistable platform to shift the emission by a very significant amount," said Gale. "Often when you control these systems, the amount of manipulation is very limited, but in this case the shift was much larger than expected.

"Rather than trying to make hBN defects behave like a traditional solid-state hosts, we took advantage of hBN's own strength: its thin, layered, twistable structure."

Why Hexagonal Boron Nitride Is Different

Gale compared the material's structure to slices of cheese rather than a solid block.

"With a block of cheese, you can't really get to the flavor in the middle. But with slices, you can peel away layers, put them back together and change how they interact," he said.

Because hBN is made of extremely thin layers, researchers can separate and reassemble those layers in ways that are not possible with more conventional quantum materials.

New Possibilities for Quantum Technologies

Supervising author Professor Igor Aharonovich said the ability to twist layered materials is particularly exciting because it can reveal entirely new physical behavior.

"You can take two layers that don't do much on their own, put them together at a specific angle, and suddenly you have a completely different system," said Professor Aharonovich.

According to Aharonovich, the findings could help advance several emerging quantum technologies.

"These materials could eventually be used for quantum computing communications and quantum sensing, which would help for applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity and improved GPS; and gives us more control over the building blocks needed to get there."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in controlling quantum emitters using a twistable layered material, hexagonal boron nitride. This advance offers a new method to tune quantum light sources, which are crucial components for future quantum computing, secure communications, and sensing technologies. The impact is broad and meaningful for the general public as it could accelerate the development of transformative quantum technologies with wide-ranging applications in healthcare, cybersecurity, and navigation. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, the experimental method, and its potential implications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Twisting layered material offers new control over quantum light
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are working to develop tiny sources of light called quantum emitters. These emitters can help build future quantum computers and better communication systems, but controlling their behavior has been difficult.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Technology Sydney found that by twisting very thin layers of a material called hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), they could change the color and wavelength of the light from quantum emitters inside it. Unlike other materials, hBN’s thin layers can be lifted, twisted, and put back together to adjust the light multiple times.

**Impact** – This method gives scientists a new way to control quantum emitters more strongly than before. Because hBN is made of thin slices, it allows changes that solid materials like diamond cannot. This could speed up the creation of quantum devices used in computing, secure communication, and sensitive sensors.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue exploring how twisting these layers affects quantum emitters. This could lead to new quantum technologies that improve healthcare tools, cybersecurity, and navigation systems like GPS.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Twisting layered sheets of hexagonal boron nitride lets scientists control quantum light in new ways, moving quantum technology closer to real-world use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une torsion des couches permet un nouveau contrôle de la lumière quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à développer de minuscules sources lumineuses appelées émetteurs quantiques. Ces émetteurs pourraient aider à construire des ordinateurs quantiques et des systèmes de communication plus performants, mais il est difficile de contrôler leur comportement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Technologie de Sydney ont découvert qu'en tordant des couches très fines d'un matériau appelé nitrure de bore hexagonal (hBN), ils pouvaient changer la couleur et la longueur d'onde de la lumière émise par ces émetteurs quantiques. Contrairement à d'autres matériaux, les couches fines de hBN peuvent être soulevées, tordues et remises en place plusieurs fois pour ajuster la lumière.

**Impact** – Cette méthode donne aux scientifiques un nouveau moyen de mieux contrôler les émetteurs quantiques. Comme le hBN est composé de fines tranches, il permet des changements impossibles avec des matériaux solides comme le diamant. Cela pourrait accélérer la création d'appareils quantiques utilisés en informatique, communication sécurisée et capteurs très sensibles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à étudier comment la torsion de ces couches influence les émetteurs quantiques. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouvelles technologies quantiques améliorant les outils de santé, la cybersécurité et les systèmes de navigation comme le GPS.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Tordre des couches de nitrure de bore hexagonal permet de contrôler la lumière quantique autrement, rapprochant les technologies quantiques d'une utilisation réelle.
articles.personality_title_es: Girar capas delgadas ofrece nuevo control sobre la luz cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan desarrollar pequeñas fuentes de luz llamadas emisores cuánticos. Estos emisores pueden ayudar a crear computadoras cuánticas y mejores sistemas de comunicación, pero controlar su comportamiento ha sido difícil.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Tecnología de Sídney descubrieron que al girar capas muy delgadas de un material llamado nitruro de boro hexagonal (hBN), podían cambiar el color y la longitud de onda de la luz emitida por estos emisores cuánticos. A diferencia de otros materiales, las capas delgadas de hBN se pueden levantar, girar y volver a colocar varias veces para ajustar la luz.

**Impacto** – Este método brinda a los científicos una nueva forma de controlar mejor los emisores cuánticos. Debido a que hBN está hecho de rebanadas delgadas, permite cambios que materiales sólidos como el diamante no pueden. Esto podría acelerar la creación de dispositivos cuánticos para computación, comunicaciones seguras y sensores muy sensibles.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán explorando cómo girar estas capas afecta a los emisores cuánticos. Esto podría conducir a nuevas tecnologías cuánticas que mejoren herramientas de salud, ciberseguridad y sistemas de navegación como el GPS.

**Resumen en una frase** – Girar capas delgadas de nitruro de boro hexagonal permite controlar la luz cuántica de nuevas maneras, acercando la tecnología cuántica a su uso real.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of delicately stacked, translucent hexagonal layers resembling thin slices of glowing cheese, gently twisted and rotated mid-air, emitting soft, colorful beams of light that shift in hue and wavelength, symbolizing the dynamic control of quantum light in layered materials.

articles.title: From malaria to energy: Why solutions from the Global South aren’t reaching the people who need them most
articles.summary: Innovators – especially in the Global South – are too often locked out of funding and opportunity despite offering solutions to some of the world’s most pressing challenges.
articles.content:
Malaria wasn’t on Masaki Umeda’s mind when his drone startup, SORA Technology, launched in 2020 in Nagoya, Japan.

Back then, he and his colleagues were focused on getting medical supplies to hard-to-reach parts of Africa but, after talking to health ministries, they realised their AI-powered solutions would be more useful in the fight against the devastating disease, which kills over half a million people on the continent every year.

© SORA Technology

“We fly drones in targeted areas and collect raw data,” explains Mr. Umeda. “Then the AI tools identify the location and characteristics of bodies of water such as turbidity (cloudiness due to the presence of particles like algae or micro-organisms), temperature ranges and nearby vegetation, all of which allow us to classify the risk of breeding sites.”

When shared with government agencies, this information enables them to instruct ground spraying companies to focus their activities on particular high-risk spots, rather than simply blanketing large-scale areas.

In a world of shrinking aid and international budgets, cost-effectiveness is a top priority for cash-strapped countries, and the solutions offered by innovators and start-ups are more important than ever.

SORA Technology’s potential to save lives (and money) led to Mr. Umeda being invited to take part in the UN’s 2026 Science and Technology Forum as a “featured innovator,” along with several other early-stage solution developers from diverse backgrounds dedicated to solving real-world challenges.

UN News

The start-ups offer a wide range of solutions, from e-waste recycling in Zambia to solar energy solutions in Argentina and community-based renewable energy hubs in Nigeria.

“The innovations point to broader lessons,” says Li Junhua, UN Under-Secretary-General for Economic and Social Affairs. “They remind us that innovation is most effective when paired with collaboration, local ownership and clear pathways to scale.”

‘Extraordinary talent’ locked out

The Featured Innovator programme is an effort by the UN to raise awareness of the wealth of talent, particularly from developing countries, that is going untapped due to limited access to finance, technology and opportunity.

“This is not a gap in innovation. It is a gap in inclusion.” Lok Bahadur Thapa, the President of the UN Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC), said at the start of the Forum.

“Too many innovators remain disconnected from finance and markets. Too many solutions do not reach the communities that need them most.”

Rita Orji knows all too well about the challenges faced by talented young people in the Global South. She is a Professor of Computer Science and the Canada Research Chair in Persuasive Technology (digital tools designed to influence attitudes or behaviour) at Dalhousie University in Canada.

Growing up in a remote village in southeastern Nigeria, without electricity or running water, she told the STI Forum that she had never seen a computer up close before she went to Nnamdi Azikiwe University.

I chose that field in the hope that it could help me change things for my community and people like me,” she said. “I spent my undergraduate years learning how to code, how to build systems and think computationally without owning a computer.” Nevertheless, she graduated with first-class honours.

© UNICEF/Olivier Asselin

Ms. Orji shared her story as an example of the many “extraordinary talents” across the Global South who, unlike her, are locked out – not because they lack ability but because they lack access.

‘Technically brilliant, developmentally useless’

Digital tools designed in the Global South, by talented individuals living and working in those countries would, Ms. Orji argues, help to ensure that they are actually effective.

“When the world talks about AI and the Global South, the story is often that of transfer. Design it in the north, deploy it in the south, eventually adapt it and eventually make it affordable. That model is backwards,” she says.

“The Global South should not be treated as a late adopter of intelligent design elsewhere. It should help lead in shaping what intelligence becomes.”

Today, most AI tools assume that users are literate, English-speaking and digitally fluent, which excludes most people on the planet. According to Ms. Orji, this makes them “technically brilliant but developmentally useless for those who need them most.”

“The question before us today is not whether the Global South is ready for the AI future, but whether the Global AI future is ready to learn from the Global South.”

Find out more about the innovators featured at the 2026 STI Forum here.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights innovative technological solutions from the Global South addressing major challenges like malaria and renewable energy, emphasizing the need for inclusion and access to scale these impactful innovations. It focuses on real-world benefits for broad populations, especially in developing countries, and provides detailed context about the innovations and barriers faced.
articles.personality_title: Innovators from the Global South create solutions but face barriers to reach those in need
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries in the Global South face serious problems like malaria and lack of energy. Local innovators have developed smart solutions, but they often struggle to get funding and support to help the people who need these solutions most.

**What happened** – SORA Technology, a Japanese startup, uses drones with artificial intelligence (AI) to find mosquito breeding sites in Africa, helping fight malaria. This approach helps health workers spray only high-risk areas, saving money and lives. The UN invited SORA and other innovators from the Global South to share their work at a 2026 Science and Technology Forum, highlighting projects from solar energy in Argentina to e-waste recycling in Zambia.

**Impact** – These innovations show how local ideas can better solve local problems. Experts say many talented people in the Global South are blocked from getting resources, not because they lack skill but due to limited access to money and markets. For example, Professor Rita Orji from Nigeria explained how AI tools designed in richer countries often don’t work well for people in poorer regions because they assume users speak English and have digital skills.

**What's next step** – The UN and partners want to improve collaboration, support local ownership, and create ways for these innovations to grow and reach more people. This means changing how funding and technology are shared so that solutions from the Global South can lead and shape their own future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Local innovators in the Global South develop important solutions for major challenges but need better access to funding and markets to help those who need them most.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les innovateurs du Sud global créent des solutions mais peinent à atteindre ceux qui en ont besoin
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays du Sud global rencontrent de graves problèmes comme le paludisme et le manque d’énergie. Les innovateurs locaux développent des solutions intelligentes, mais ils ont souvent du mal à obtenir des financements et du soutien pour aider les personnes qui en ont le plus besoin.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – SORA Technology, une startup japonaise, utilise des drones avec intelligence artificielle (IA) pour repérer les lieux de reproduction des moustiques en Afrique, aidant ainsi à lutter contre le paludisme. Cette méthode permet aux agents de santé de pulvériser uniquement les zones à haut risque, économisant ainsi de l’argent et des vies. L’ONU a invité SORA et d’autres innovateurs du Sud global à présenter leurs travaux lors du Forum scientifique et technologique 2026, mettant en lumière des projets allant de l’énergie solaire en Argentine au recyclage des déchets électroniques en Zambie.

**Impact** – Ces innovations montrent que les idées locales peuvent mieux résoudre les problèmes locaux. Des experts expliquent que de nombreux talents du Sud global sont bloqués non par manque de compétences, mais par un accès limité aux financements et aux marchés. Par exemple, la professeure Rita Orji du Nigeria a expliqué que les outils d’IA conçus dans les pays riches ne fonctionnent souvent pas bien pour les populations plus pauvres, car ils supposent que les utilisateurs parlent anglais et maîtrisent le numérique.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU et ses partenaires veulent renforcer la collaboration, encourager la propriété locale et créer des moyens pour que ces innovations se développent et atteignent plus de personnes. Cela implique de changer la manière dont les financements et la technologie sont partagés, afin que les solutions du Sud global puissent diriger et façonner leur propre avenir.

**Une phrase clé** – Les innovateurs locaux du Sud global développent des solutions importantes pour des défis majeurs, mais ont besoin d’un meilleur accès aux financements et aux marchés pour aider ceux qui en ont le plus besoin.
articles.personality_title_es: Innovadores del Sur global crean soluciones pero enfrentan barreras para llegar a quienes las necesitan
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países del Sur global enfrentan problemas graves como la malaria y la falta de energía. Los innovadores locales han desarrollado soluciones inteligentes, pero a menudo tienen dificultades para obtener financiamiento y apoyo para ayudar a las personas que más lo necesitan.

**Qué pasó** – SORA Technology, una startup japonesa, usa drones con inteligencia artificial (IA) para encontrar criaderos de mosquitos en África, ayudando a combatir la malaria. Este método permite a los trabajadores de salud rociar solo las áreas de alto riesgo, ahorrando dinero y vidas. La ONU invitó a SORA y otros innovadores del Sur global a compartir sus proyectos en el Foro de Ciencia y Tecnología 2026, destacando iniciativas desde energía solar en Argentina hasta reciclaje de residuos electrónicos en Zambia.

**Impacto** – Estas innovaciones muestran cómo las ideas locales pueden resolver mejor los problemas locales. Expertos dicen que muchos talentos del Sur global están bloqueados no por falta de habilidad, sino por acceso limitado a dinero y mercados. Por ejemplo, la profesora Rita Orji de Nigeria explicó que las herramientas de IA diseñadas en países ricos a menudo no funcionan bien para personas en regiones pobres porque asumen que los usuarios hablan inglés y tienen habilidades digitales.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU y sus socios quieren mejorar la colaboración, apoyar la propiedad local y crear formas para que estas innovaciones crezcan y lleguen a más personas. Esto significa cambiar cómo se comparten los fondos y la tecnología para que las soluciones del Sur global puedan liderar y dar forma a su propio futuro.

**Una frase clave** – Los innovadores locales del Sur global desarrollan soluciones importantes para grandes desafíos, pero necesitan mejor acceso a financiamiento y mercados para ayudar a quienes más lo necesitan.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a drone gently flying over a stylized African landscape dotted with shimmering water pools, where glowing data streams flow from the water into the drone, symbolizing AI-powered analysis; nearby, interconnected abstract shapes represent diverse innovators from the Global South collaborating and bridging gaps, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues.
